does: This paper introduces Critic-RM, a framework that augments reward modeling for language models by having them jointly predict numerical reward scores and generate natural language critiques explaining those scores. The method uses a two-stage approach: first generating and filtering high-quality critiques from model predictions without additional human annotation, then fine-tuning the reward model on both reward prediction and critique generation objectives simultaneously. Experiments demonstrate that this approach improves reward modeling accuracy by 3.7%-7.3% over standard scalar-only reward models and LLM judges across multiple benchmarks, while also showing gains of 2.5%-3.2% in improving reasoning accuracy when critiques are used to identify and correct flawed reasoning steps.
connects: This work builds on the foundation of reward modeling in reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), which has become central to aligning large language models with human preferences. The paper extends prior work that treats reward modeling as a simple scalar prediction task by incorporating ideas from mechanistic interpretability and explainability research, which have emphasized the value of natural language explanations for model decisions. It responds to limitations of existing approaches that struggle to incorporate structured reasoning and feedback, and relates to recent work on using language models to generate their own supervision signals and self-critique frameworks that have shown promise in improving model behavior without additional human labeling.
enables: This framework opens several promising directions for improving alignment and interpretability of language models. The demonstrated effectiveness of joint prediction tasks suggests future work could explore similar multi-objective training strategies for other model outputs, such as uncertainty estimates or step-by-step reasoning chains. The self-generated critique approach without extra supervision could scale to larger datasets and domains where human annotation is expensive, potentially improving RLHF pipelines in practice. Additionally, the ability to generate interpretable critiques alongside reward predictions enables better transparency in model decisions, supporting development of more trustworthy AI systems and facilitating human oversight of reward models themselves, which remains an open challenge in alignment research.
